guidance.text:
Metformin AccuRx:
• Part 1 - Dosing Instructions
• Part 2 - Side Effect Expectation

Symptoms of Diabetes:
• Lethargy
• Confusion
• Increased Thirst
• Increased Urinary Frequency
• Increased Urinary Volume

A1c Control
Control is judged via 3 monthly A1c checks.

Targets are below:
1 med: A1c 48 or below
2 meds: A1c 53 or below
3 meds: A1c 58 or below
guidance.location: purple box, left side, below Symptoms of Diabetes
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would assess glycaemic control status based on current treatment intensity and latest 3‑monthly HbA1c/A1c result. Inputs: number of diabetes medications currently used (1, 2, or 3) and most recent A1c value from the scheduled 3‑monthly check. Logic: determine the applicable threshold by medication count; if on 1 medication, target is A1c 48 or below; if on 2 medications, target is A1c 53 or below; if on 3 medications, target is A1c 58 or below. Compare the measured A1c to the threshold. Output: controlled vs not controlled, plus the threshold used. This output can feed treatment escalation steps such as whether to continue current therapy or move to dual/triple therapy or insulin referral.

guidance.text:
Practical Advice
Most patients with T2DM will have a BMI ≥ 25. As such Gliclazide and Pioglitazone should be lower priority than Gliptin.

Most patients will be at "high risk" for CVD using below "CVD Known or Risk" criteria, and would qualify for Metformin ->SGLT2i in succession.

Metformin Tolerability**
Metformin can only be deemed intolerable if modified release has been tried. Helps weight loss. Should be up titrated to 2g daily in 500mg increments.

Being on some metformin, is better than NO metformin.
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would determine whether metformin is tolerated and how to titrate it. Inputs: current metformin formulation, prior exposure to standard vs modified release metformin, current dose, adverse effects, and whether patient is taking any metformin at all. Logic: first verify whether intolerance has been adequately assessed; metformin should only be classified as intolerable if modified release has been tried. If tolerated, up‑titrate metformin in 500 mg increments until reaching 2 g daily as tolerated. Consider beneficial effects such as weight loss. If full target dose is not achievable but some metformin is tolerated, retain partial metformin rather than stopping completely. Output: one of tolerated and continue titration; partially tolerated and continue partial dose; or truly intolerable after modified release trial, which would support switching away from metformin.

guidance.text:
SGLT2i (for diabetes)
Risk of DKA - counsel on normoglycaemic ketosi
• Helps weight loss
• Increases UTI/thrush risk
No A1c reduction if eGFR <45
Avoid if A1c >85 or Age >85

SGLT2i (for CVD/CKD)
Diabetic kidney disease:
Best choice empagliflozin 10mg OD, used until eGFR 20.

Heart Failure without Diabetes:
Dapagliflozin 10mg OD can be used until eGFR 15.
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would support SGLT2 inhibitor selection and appropriateness checking across diabetes, cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, and heart failure contexts. Inputs: indication (diabetes vs CVD/CKD vs heart failure without diabetes), eGFR, A1c, age, DKA risk/counselling status, and comorbidity profile. Logic: if using for diabetes, counsel about normoglycaemic ketosis/DKA risk, note weight-loss benefit and UTI/thrush risk, and recognize reduced A1c effect if eGFR <45; avoid for glycaemic indication if A1c >85 or age >85. If diabetic kidney disease is present, prefer empagliflozin 10 mg once daily and continue until eGFR 20. If heart failure without diabetes, dapagliflozin 10 mg once daily can be used until eGFR 15. Output: recommended SGLT2i agent, dose, eligibility/avoidance status, and key counselling/safety messages.

guidance.text:
Gliclazide
• Main benefit, fast A1c reduction
• Risk of Hypos - need testing kit
• Causes Weight Gain
guidance.location: green box, bottom far left lower section

guidance.text:
Gliptins
Not to be used with GLP-1, pancreatitis risk.
• Weight neutral
• "Linagliptin" usable at low eGFR
guidance.location: green box, bottom far left lower section

guidance.text:
Pioglitazone
• Causes Weight Gain
• Only real use is for delaying Insulin - Taxi/HGV Drivers
Contraindicated in:
• HF
• Osteopenia/porosis in women
• bladder cancer
• Patients taking Insulin
guidance.location: green box, bottom left-center lower section
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would determine whether pioglitazone is an appropriate option. Inputs: patient occupation/clinical reason for delaying insulin, heart failure status, bone health history especially osteopenia/osteoporosis in women, bladder cancer history, current insulin use, and concern about weight gain. Logic: consider pioglitazone mainly when there is a need to delay insulin, especially in Taxi/HGV drivers. Before use, screen contraindications: heart failure, osteopenia/osteoporosis in women, bladder cancer, and concomitant insulin use. Also factor in expected weight gain. Output: suitable vs contraindicated vs low-priority option, with documented reason.

guidance.text: Minimum BMI for GLP Initiation is 23
guidance.location: purple box, right side above center

guidance.text:
Scheduling Follow-up
After a review and dose change, patient should have A1c requested on T-Quest and sent scheduled self-booking link for:
• Bloods in 12 weeks
• Follow up review in 14 weeks.
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would generate post‑treatment-change follow-up actions. Inputs: confirmation that a review and dose change occurred, patient identity/contact method, and ordering/scheduling systems availability. Logic: after any review with dose change, request A1c through T‑Quest, then send the patient a scheduled self‑booking link. The protocol schedules blood tests at 12 weeks and follow-up review at 14 weeks. Output: ordered A1c, patient notification/self-booking instructions sent, and dated follow-up milestones for bloods and review.

guidance.text:
CVD Known or Risk*
Heart Failure
CKD
IHD
QRISK >10%

<40 years with any of:
• HTN
• High Lipids
• Smoking
• BMI >25
• FHx CVD
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would classify whether a patient meets the chart's "CVD Known or Risk" definition. Inputs: history of heart failure, CKD, IHD, QRISK score, age, hypertension status, lipid status, smoking status, BMI, and family history of cardiovascular disease. Logic: mark positive if any of the following are present: heart failure, CKD, IHD, or QRISK >10%. Also mark positive if the patient is under 40 years old and has any one of the listed factors: hypertension, high lipids, smoking, BMI >25, or family history of CVD. Output: binary classification of "CVD Known or Risk" yes/no, with the triggering criterion documented. This output routes the patient toward the CVD-risk treatment branch.

guidance.text:
Sick Day Rules:
Stop following meds when sick:
• S: SGLT2i
• A: ACEI
• D: Diuretics
• M: Metformin
• A: ARB
• N: NSAIDs

Patient info:
https://www.diabetes.co.uk/sick-day-rules.html
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would provide acute-illness medication holding guidance. Inputs: current medication list and whether the patient is currently unwell/sick. Logic: if sick, identify medications matching the SADMAN mnemonic and advise temporary stopping of SGLT2 inhibitors, ACE inhibitors, diuretics, metformin, ARBs, and NSAIDs. Provide the patient information resource link. Output: a personalized list of medications to hold during illness and patient education guidance.

guidance.text:
Driving:
Go through driving education with patient, regarding DVLA rules.

Information to share with patients:
https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/information-for-drivers-with-diabetes

Prognostic Value
High Value Cases:
• Any CVD Risk Factors in the Red Box
• Established CVD Disease
• Known CKD
guidance.subworkflow_brief: This subworkflow would stratify patients into high prognostic value cases for GLP-1 pathway selection. Inputs: presence of any CVD risk factors listed in the red "CVD Known or Risk" box, established cardiovascular disease, and known CKD. Logic: classify as high value if any red-box CVD risk factor is present, or if established CVD disease is present, or if known CKD is present. If none apply, classify as low prognostic value. Output: prognostic value category (high vs low), which can then direct the choice between agents such as Ozempic vs Mounjaro.

root_node.label: Treatment Algorithm
outgoing_edges: Downward arrow labeled "Not at risk of CVD" leads to "Metformin"
Rightward arrow labeled "*CVD Known or Risk" leads to "Metformin"
